name: Arsenic
name_origin: From the Greek 'arsenikon', the name for the yellow mineral orpiment (As2S3); the Greek term itself likely derives from a Persian word for the same pigment.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Arsenic is the 53rd most abundant element in Earth's crust at about 1.8 ppm. It is widely distributed and occurs both as the native element and in many sulfide minerals; major producers include China, Morocco, and Russia. Most commercial arsenic is recovered as a by-product of copper, gold, and lead smelting.

compounds.name: Arsenic trioxide
compounds.summary: White, highly toxic solid used historically as a poison and pigment; today an FDA-approved chemotherapy drug for acute promyelocytic leukemia.

compounds.name: Arsine
compounds.summary: Colorless, extremely toxic gas used as a dopant precursor in the semiconductor industry and historically in the Marsh test for arsenic.

compounds.name: Gallium arsenide
compounds.summary: III-V semiconductor with a direct bandgap, used in high-frequency electronics, infrared LEDs, laser diodes, and high-efficiency solar cells.

compounds.name: Orpiment (arsenic trisulfide)
compounds.summary: Yellow mineral used since antiquity as a pigment ('king's yellow') and now as an infrared-transmitting chalcogenide glass component.

compounds.name: Arsenic acid
compounds.summary: Strong oxidizing acid used in the manufacture of arsenate salts and historically as a wood preservative (CCA-treated lumber).

uses: Doping agent for III-V semiconductors and silicon
Lead alloying additive for car batteries and ammunition
Wood preservatives (chromated copper arsenate, now restricted)
Pesticides and herbicides (largely phased out due to toxicity)
Chemotherapy (arsenic trioxide for acute promyelocytic leukemia)
reactivity_summary: Arsenic is a brittle metalloid with several allotropes; the stable gray form is metallic-looking and a poor conductor. It tarnishes in air and burns when heated to give the trioxide with a characteristic garlic odor. Arsenic does not react with water or non-oxidizing acids but dissolves in nitric acid and hot concentrated sulfuric acid. Most arsenic compounds are highly toxic to humans and animals.
isotopes_summary: Arsenic has only one stable isotope, 75As, making it a mononuclidic element. About 33 radioisotopes are known; the longest-lived is 73As (half-life ~80.3 days), and 74As (half-life ~17.8 days) is used as a positron-emitting tracer.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
